title: Pithecellobium forfex
intro: Pithecellobium forfex, also known as the "Gum Tree" or "Talisay", is a tropical legume tree native to Southeast Asia, commonly found in the Philippines and other parts of the region. This plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, and antifungal activities, contributing to its use in treating various health conditions, such as fever, wounds, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Pithecellobium forfex include flavonoids, saponins, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Pithecellobium forfex is often prepared as a decoction or a powder and used to treat various ailments, making it a popular ingredient in herbal remedies such as teas, infusions, and topical creams.
taxonomy: Pithecellobium forfex is classified as a species of legume tree in the genus Pithecellobium. The genus Pithecellobium belongs to the family Fabaceae, commonly known as the pea or bean family. This classification is based on its morphological characteristics and genetic analysis. Further, Pithecellobium forfex is a part of the subfamily Faboideae, which includes a wide range of flowering plants. The classification of Pithecellobium forfex is essential in understanding its evolutionary history and relationships with other plant species.

parts: Pithecellobium forfex, also known as the "slipper catechu" or "tree catechu", is a plant used in traditional medicine. The bark of this plant is a significant source of medicinal properties. preparations: Pithecellobium forfex, also known as the "tamarind tree" or "ninegroove tamarind," has been used in traditional herbal preparations for various purposes.
